title: The field knows what it wants
why.no-email-type: The email field is still type="text". Meaning: "I accept anything at all". Change it to email and the browser checks it itself, and phones show a keyboard with an @.
why.no-tel-type: The phone is still text. On a mobile the person gets a letter keyboard and has to hunt for the digits. type="tel" opens the number pad.
why.no-required: Nothing is required. A person can send an empty form and you receive nothing. One attribute — and the browser refuses to submit.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: Three fields. All of them type="text". Meaning: "I accept anything at all".
blocks.1.text: Someone will type "dunno" in the email. They will type the letter "o" instead of a zero in the phone. And you will receive rubbish.
blocks.3.items.1: email — must have an @ and a dot after it.
blocks.3.items.2: tel — a phone number. On mobile it opens the number pad.
blocks.3.items.3: number — digits only. It also takes min and max.
blocks.3.items.4: date — a calendar. Not text, an actual calendar.
blocks.3.items.5: password — what you type is hidden behind dots.
blocks.4.text: And more: url, search, color, time, file, range.
blocks.6.text: Change type="text" to type="email" and see what you get for free.
blocks.7.text: The browser checks it itself — type something without an @ and the form will not submit, and the person sees a message. You wrote not a single line for that.
blocks.8.text: On a phone a keyboard opens with the @ in plain sight. The person does not hunt for it.
blocks.11.text: required is an attribute with no value — it simply exists. It means: without this field the form does not go.
blocks.12.text: Try it: leave it empty and press the button. The browser stops the submission and points at the field.
blocks.13.text: Again, zero lines of code from you.
blocks.15.text: Here is the important part many people miss: this check is a CONVENIENCE, not a defence.
blocks.16.text: Anyone can open the browser tools and remove required with two clicks. Or send the data directly, without ever opening your page.
blocks.17.text: The browser check protects a person from their own mistake. It does not protect you from a bad person.
blocks.18.text: So the server checks everything AGAIN. Always. Without exception.
blocks.19.text: The rule: check in the browser for convenience. Check on the server for security. Never only one.
blocks.20.text: Your turn. Give each field the right type and make the email required.
blocks.21.text: And if you are on a phone — try it before and after. You will feel the difference with your fingers.
blocks.22.text: But something is still missing. Your fields have no labels — there are paragraphs above them, which is not the same thing. Next lesson.
